title: Rabbit myxomatosis and RHD: vaccination and prevention
subtitle: The two diseases that kill domestic rabbits rapidly, and the vaccines that prevent both.
excerpt: Myxomatosis and rabbit haemorrhagic disease (RHD) are both fatal in unvaccinated rabbits and both present in the wild rabbit population throughout the UK. Combined vaccination is available and covers both strains of RHD, but timing and booster intervals matter. This tutorial covers what the diseases are, how they spread, and how to set up a vaccination programme.
sourceNotes: British Veterinary Association, rabbit vaccination guidance. MRCVS vaccination schedules for Nobivac Myxo-RHD Plus. Rabbit Welfare Association and Fund, disease prevention guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: A viral disease of rabbits caused by Myxoma virus, spread by biting insects (fleas, mosquitoes, rabbit fleas) and direct contact. Causes severe swelling around the eyes, nose, and genitals. Fatal in unvaccinated rabbits within 10-12 days in most cases.

A highly contagious viral disease of rabbits. RHD1 has been present in Europe since the 1980s; RHD2 arrived in the UK around 2015 and is now widespread. Both cause rapid fatal illness; RHD2 can kill within 24-36 hours, sometimes with no visible symptoms.

A single subcutaneous injection covering myxomatosis and both RHD1 and RHD2 strains (Nobivac Myxo-RHD Plus). Requires annual boosters; some vets recommend 6-monthly boosters in high-risk areas where wild rabbit contact is possible.

content.text: are present in UK wild rabbits and fatal to unvaccinated domestic stock. Any outdoor rabbit near wild rabbits, or in housing where fleas can pass between the two, is at real risk.

(Nobivac Myxo-RHD Plus) covers myxomatosis, RHD1, and RHD2 in a single subcutaneous injection. Rabbits can be vaccinated from 5 weeks of age. Annual boosters are required to maintain immunity. In outdoor systems where wild rabbit contact is possible or where insect pressure is high, some vets recommend 6-monthly boosters through the summer period.

The vaccine requires a vet prescription and is given by a vet or registered nurse. For a large colony, discuss with your vet whether a programme covering breeding does and replacement stock is cost-effective. Rabbits slaughtered at 8-12 weeks need not be vaccinated unless kept beyond slaughter weight.

Beyond vaccination, reduce risk by: fitting insect-proof mesh to outdoor hutches and runs; using a flea product licensed for rabbits; preventing wild rabbit access by fencing. RHD2 can travel on clothing, boots, and equipment; disinfect footwear after handling wild rabbits.

Myxomatosis is hard to miss: severe facial swelling and discharge develop over 5-10 days. RHD2 may show no signs before death; a rabbit found dead with no prior illness in an unvaccinated colony should prompt immediate veterinary contact. There is no treatment for either disease; euthanasia is the welfare decision for affected animals.
